Author: Pedro Falcato <pfalcato@suse.de>

    mm/mprotect: special-case small folios when applying write permissions
    
    The common order-0 case is important enough to want its own branch, and
    avoids the hairy, large loop logic that the CPU does not seem to handle
    particularly well.
    
    While at it, encourage the compiler to inline batch PTE logic and resolve
    constant branches by adding __always_inline strategically.
    
    Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>
    Signed-off-by: Pedro Falcato <pfalcato@suse.de>

First, the core of the change: The addition of `__always_inline` to `mprotect_folio_pte_batch`, `prot_commit_flush_ptes`, and `commit_anon_folio_batch`. The intent is to reduce overhead by inlining these functions, especially for the critical path. Then, there's the specific optimization within `commit_anon_folio_batch`: handling the case where `nr_ptes == 1`. In this common scenario (a single page being mprotected), the code now directly calls `prot_commit_flush_ptes` with the `PageAnonExclusive` bit of the *first* page passed as the `set_write` argument.

The key questions arise from this fast path. Does it behave *identically* to the original loop? It needs to. I checked that it is identical.

`commit_anon_folio_batch` is called from `set_write_prot_commit_flush_ptes`.
First, `set_write_prot_commit_flush_ptes` checked `maybe_change_pte_writable` and `folio_test_anon(folio)`, so if `PageAnonExclusive(first_page)` is true, it is safe to make it writable. The logic to determine if we can modify the page is in `can_change_private_pte_writable`.

The logic here hinges on whether the `PageAnonExclusive` bit is handled correctly in this fast path, especially when dealing with large folios. But `PageAnonExclusive` is per-page and correct because it is PTE-mapped. It's a key bit in determining write permission. Given this, it makes perfect sense because it simply reduces loop. We are iterating through PTEs. The page can be the tail page and has the proper behavior.

The original code iterates to check `PageAnonExclusive` per page to batch them, and the optimized code simply applies the check to `first_page` for the single-page case. They are the same. I checked the arguments passed to `prot_commit_flush_ptes`: `vma, addr, ptep, oldpte, ptent, nr_ptes, idx, set_write, tlb`. So, the fast path looks fine. `prot_commit_flush_ptes` is handling the write protection logic correctly. The TLB flushing also. The inline expansions will be fine. All looks good here!
